# JSON File Observer

<!-- status autogenerated section -->
| Status        |           |
| ------------- |-----------|
| Stability     | [alpha]  |
| Distributions | [contrib] |

[alpha]: https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-collector/blob/main/docs/component-stability.md#alpha
[contrib]: https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-collector-releases/tree/main/distributions/otelcol-contrib
<!-- end autogenerated section -->

The `jsonfile_observer` is a [Receiver Creator](../../../receiver/receivercreator/README.md)-compatible "watch observer" that will detect and report
endpoints from a JSON file. The observer watches the file for changes and automatically updates endpoints when the file is modified.

## Example Config

```yaml
extensions:
  jsonfile_observer:
    path: /etc/otel/endpoints.json
    refresh_interval: 10s

receivers:
  receiver_creator:
    watch_observers: [jsonfile_observer]
    receivers:
      redis:
        rule: type == "jsonfile" && labels["service"] == "redis"
        config:
          endpoint: '`endpoint`'
          password: '`labels["password"]`'
```

## JSON File Format

The JSON file should contain an array of endpoint objects:

```json
[
  {
    "id": "redis-primary",
    "target": "redis-primary.example.com:6379",
    "name": "Primary Redis",
    "labels": {
      "service": "redis",
      "role": "primary",
      "password": "secret123"
    }
  },
  {
    "id": "redis-replica",
    "target": "redis-replica.example.com:6379",
    "name": "Replica Redis",
    "labels": {
      "service": "redis",
      "role": "replica",
      "password": "secret456"
    }
  }
]
```

### Endpoint Fields

| Field  | Type              | Required | Description |
|--------|-------------------|----------|-------------|
| id     | string            | No       | Unique identifier for the endpoint. If not provided, the target is used as the ID. |
| target | string            | Yes      | The endpoint target (e.g., hostname:port). |
| name   | string            | No       | A human-readable name for the endpoint. |
| labels | map[string]string | No       | Arbitrary key-value pairs for filtering and configuration. |

## Config

| Name             | Type     | Default | Description |
|------------------|----------|---------|-------------|
| path             | string   | (required) | Path to the JSON file containing endpoint configurations. |
| refresh_interval | duration | `10s`   | How often to check for file changes. |

## Endpoint Details

When an endpoint is discovered from the JSON file, the following fields are available for use in receiver creator rules:

| Field    | Type              | Description |
|----------|-------------------|-------------|
| id       | string            | The endpoint ID. |
| name     | string            | The endpoint name. |
| labels   | map[string]string | The endpoint labels. |
| endpoint | string            | The endpoint target. |
| type     | string            | Always "jsonfile". |

## Dynamic Updates

The observer watches the JSON file for changes. When the file is modified:
- New endpoints are added
- Removed endpoints are deleted
- Modified endpoints are updated

This allows for dynamic endpoint management without restarting the collector.

## Use Cases

- **Service discovery from configuration management**: Load endpoints from files managed by Ansible, Puppet, Chef, or other configuration management tools.
- **Static service lists**: Define a static list of services to monitor that doesn't require Kubernetes or other service discovery mechanisms.
- **Testing and development**: Easily configure test endpoints for development environments.
- **Integration with external systems**: Have external systems write endpoint configurations that the collector picks up automatically.
